one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-and-architecture survey of FL security and privacy that maps poisoning, adversarial, and inference threats plus proactive/reactive defenses onto a proposed three-layer FL architecture, concluding that the security-vs-accuracy/efficiency trade-off remains the central unsolved challenge.
key_finding: The recurring cross-cutting conclusion is that every FL defense faces an unresolved trade-off between security/privacy and model accuracy/efficiency: encryption (HE, MPC/secret sharing) protects data but inflates computation and communication cost; differential-privacy obfuscation degrades accuracy if noise is too high yet leaks if too low; adversarial training and diffusion-based denoising are effective but computationally taxing and incomplete. The authors also stress that no reliable method yet exists to quantitatively assess an FL system's risk, motivating their proposed direction of 'dynamic adaptive defense' and lightweight cryptography, and they flag large models (e.g., LLMs, 1.75T-parameter WuDao cited as context) as an emerging privacy/poisoning risk surface within FL.
core_contribution: A survey (to appear in KAIS) of security and privacy in Federated Learning (FL). Its own novel contributions are three synthesis artifacts, not new algorithms or experiments: (1) a proposed 'universal' three-layer FL system architecture (Infrastructure, Algorithm, User Service) abstracted from reviewing six open-source FL frameworks, which the authors argue prior surveys lack; (2) an organized taxonomy of FL threats spanning non-malicious failures and malicious attacks (poisoning, adversarial, inference), mapped by attacker role and by the architecture layer they target; and (3) a catalog of defense techniques (proactive vs. reactive) tied to each attack class, plus an application-by-sector risk discussion and future research directions. It is a literature synthesis, not a primary experimental study.
threat_model: The paper frames FL adversaries by role (Table 1): a passive 'Spectator' that collects sensitive info from system interactions; malicious 'Devices/clients' that can leak or alter their submodel or leak local training data; and malicious/compromised 'Servers' that can leak model info or destroy global/local model availability. Threats are split into non-malicious failures (hardware faults, noisy data, unreliable/dropout participants, DoS exposure) and malicious attacks: data poisoning (label-flip, clean-label) and model poisoning (e.g., backdoors), adversarial attacks (white/gray/black-box, e.g., FGSM/I-FGSM/DeepFool/JSMA), and inference attacks (property inference and membership inference, including GAN-based reconstruction of client data by a compromised server). FL's non-IID heterogeneity and the server's lack of visibility into local training are highlighted as amplifying poisoning and detection difficulty.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no experiments, so effectiveness claims for the cataloged defenses are inherited from cited papers rather than independently measured or compared. Scope is deliberately narrowed to Horizontal FL (HFL), largely setting aside Vertical FL and Federated Transfer Learning. The proposed universal architecture is conceptual and not validated against systems beyond the six frameworks reviewed. The defense tables list method names, years, and one-line descriptions but no comparative performance, so a reader cannot rank techniques from this paper. Several future-direction sections (dynamic adaptive defense, lightweight cryptography, performance efficiency) are stated as open problems without concrete proposals.

quantitative_results: No experimental results are reported; the paper is a survey. Only cited contextual statistics appear, e.g., privacy legislation is estimated to cover up to 75% of the global population and require ~80% of worldwide enterprises to comply by 2023 (attributed to cited sources, not measured here), and the WuDao model's 1.75 trillion parameters is cited to illustrate large-model risk.
